codeReview.summary: Git.run now drains stdout/stderr CONCURRENTLY with the child via FileHandle.readabilityHandler writing into lock-guarded ByteAccumulator buffers; a post-termination readToEnd() flushes any bytes still in the pipe buffer after EOF. Implementation is clean, small (~50 lines added vs pre-fix), and honors the existing public contract. Cancellation still flows through CancelFlag → process.terminate() → GitError.cancelled. Git.stream is untouched (already had the readabilityHandler pattern). The new GitRunLargeOutputTests runs a ~315 KB commit through git show HEAD under a 10 s withTimeout — pre-fix this test deadlocks forever; post-fix completes in ~0.2 s. VAL-NET-008/010, VAL-PARSE-007, VAL-SEC-004/005 all still backed by unchanged tests. Unblocks VAL-DIFF-006 paths downstream.

issues.description: finalizeAccumulators' trailing readToEnd() uses try? so an error during the final flush is silently discarded. In practice the child is already dead and the handle EOF is guaranteed, so readToEnd() only throws on pathological fd states — but AGENTS.md observability rule says ANY swallowed error is logged, not dropped. Consider `if let tail = (try? read) ... else logger.error(...)` for symmetry with the rest of the codebase.

ByteAccumulator is `final class + NSLock + @unchecked Sendable`. The @unchecked justification doc-comment is present (cites actor-based alternatives rejected because the terminationHandler runs on a random DispatchQueue and can't await). Clear and correct. A future migration to `os_unfair_lock` would be slightly cheaper but the difference here is negligible; noting for reference.

The handoff correctly flags a theoretical pre-emption race — if Foundation ever changes so terminationHandler fires BEFORE all readability callbacks have landed, tail bytes between the last callback and the final readToEnd() could be lost. The defensive readToEnd() covers the common case (EOF not yet observed), but not simultaneous pending readability callbacks. No known issue today; flagged only because the comment on ByteAccumulator explicitly names this assumption.

runHandlesLargeStderr has a lengthy inline comment explaining why it can't easily drive stderr > 64 KB deterministically across git versions, then falls back to a second shape of large-stdout command (git log --stat -p). The chosen compromise is sensible but leaves stderr-specific coverage as a theoretical gap. Acceptable — the drain path is symmetric per-pipe, so the stdout coverage structurally proves stderr too.

ownership.notes: git-engine-worker territory (Sources/Git/*, Tests/KiteTests/GitTests/*). No swiftui-worker files touched.
